title: Neurosurgical Anesthesia MCQ – Chapter 27
source: Short Textbook of Anesthesia – Ajay Yadav

questions.question: What is cerebral perfusion pressure (CPP)?

options.A: Difference between systolic and diastolic pressure
options.B: Difference between mean arterial pressure and intracranial pressure

questions.explanation: Cerebral perfusion pressure (CPP) is the difference between mean arterial pressure and intracranial pressure.

questions.question: What is the normal cerebral blood flow (CBF) per 100 g of brain tissue?
questions.explanation: CBF is 50 mL/100 g of brain tissue.

questions.question: Between what range of mean arterial pressure is CBF maintained at constant level by autoregulation?
questions.explanation: CBF is maintained at constant level by autoregulation when mean arterial pressure is between 60 mm Hg and 150 mm Hg.

questions.question: Which of the following can abolish cerebral autoregulatory mechanism?

options.B: Cerebral ischemia, head injury, hypercarbia, hypoxia, brain edema and inhalational agents

questions.explanation: Cerebral ischemia, head injury, hypercarbia, hypoxia, brain edema and inhalational agents can abolish this autoregulatory mechanism.

questions.question: By how much does each mm increase in pCO2 above 40 mm Hg increase CBF?
questions.explanation: Each mm increase in pCO2 above 40 mm Hg increases CBF by 15-30 mL/min.

questions.question: When does pO2 increase cerebral blood flow?

options.A: When pO2 is above 100 mm Hg
options.B: Only if pO2 is below 60 mm Hg
options.C: When pO2 is between 60-80 mm Hg

questions.explanation: pO2 increases CBF only if pO2 is below 60 mm Hg.

questions.question: Which IV anesthetics decrease cerebral blood flow?

options.B: All inhalational agents, ketamine and opioids increase CBF while barbiturates, propofol, etomidate and benzodiazepines decrease CBF

questions.explanation: All inhalational agents, ketamine and opioids increase CBF while propofol, etomidate and benzodiazepines decreases the CBF.

questions.question: What increases the cerebral metabolic rate (CMR)?
questions.explanation: Hyperthermia, seizures increase the cerebral metabolic rate (CMR) while hypothermia and anesthetics decreases the cerebral metabolic rate.

questions.question: What is the normal intracranial tension (ICT)?
questions.explanation: Normal ICT is 10-15 mm Hg.

questions.question: At what level does ICT become significant and need aggressive treatment?
questions.explanation: ICT > 20 mm Hg is significant and needs aggressive treatment.

questions.question: What are the signs of raised ICT?

options.B: Headache, vomiting, altered sensorium, hypertension and bradycardia (Cushing reflex), papilledema, seizures, apnea, unconsciousness and death

questions.explanation: Signs include headache, vomiting, altered sensorium, hypertension and bradycardia (Cushing reflex), papilledema, seizures, apnea, unconsciousness and death.

questions.question: What midline shift on CT scan indicates very high ICT?
questions.explanation: At very high ICT, midline shift > 0.5 cm on computed tomography (CT) scan is seen.

questions.question: What is the most rapid method of reducing ICT?
questions.explanation: Hyperventilation is the most rapid method of reducing ICT. It reduces ICT by causing hypocapnia which causes cerebral vasoconstriction to decrease CSF production.

questions.question: What is the recommended pCO2 range to maintain during hyperventilation for reducing ICT?
questions.explanation: It is recommended to maintain pCO2 between 25-30 mm Hg. Excessive hypocapnia (< 25 mm Hg) can cause cerebral ischemia.

questions.question: What is the most commonly used hyperosmotic drug for reducing ICT?
questions.explanation: Among hyperosmotic agents, mannitol is most commonly used. These drugs are retained in intravascular compartment increasing plasma osmolarity, drawing water from brain tissue.

questions.question: Why should the use of mannitol in head injury and stroke be judicious?

options.B: If blood brain barrier is disrupted, mannitol can enter brain tissue increasing cerebral edema

questions.explanation: If blood brain barrier is disrupted (head injuries, stroke) these agents can enter the brain tissue increasing the cerebral edema. Therefore, the use of mannitol in head injury and stroke should be judicious.

questions.question: Which diuretic is very effective for reducing ICT particularly when blood brain barrier is disrupted?
questions.explanation: Furosemide is a very effective method of reducing ICT particularly when blood brain barrier is disrupted.

questions.question: How do barbiturates (Thiopentone) reduce ICT?

options.B: By causing cerebral vasoconstriction which decreases CSF production

questions.explanation: Barbiturates: Thiopentone by causing cerebral vasoconstriction is effective in decreasing the production of CSF and hence ICT.

questions.question: Which posture helps decrease ICT by increasing venous drainage?
questions.explanation: Head up position by increasing venous drainage decreases ICT.

questions.question: What can increase ICT by compressing jugular veins?
questions.explanation: Neck flexion by compressing jugular veins can increase ICT.

questions.question: If ICT is not controlled by conservative measures, how should CSF be drained?

options.B: Through ventricles (never through lumbar puncture as brain herniation can cause death)

questions.explanation: CSF drainage: If ICT is not controlled by conservative measure it should be drained through ventricles (never through lumbar puncture, brain herniation can even cause death).

questions.question: What is the effect of all inhalational agents on cerebral blood flow and ICT?

options.B: All inhalational agents (including nitrous oxide) increase CBF and hence ICT

questions.explanation: All inhalational agents (including nitrous oxide) increases the cerebral blood flow and hence ICT.

questions.question: What is the order of vasodilating potency of inhalational agents?
questions.explanation: The order of vasodilating potency is halothane >> Desflurane ≈ Isoflurane > Sevoflurane.

questions.question: By how much can Isoflurane and Sevoflurane decrease cerebral metabolic rate (CMR)?
questions.explanation: Isoflurane and Sevoflurane can decrease CMR up to 50%.

questions.question: Which inhalational agent is preferred for neurosurgery in current day practice?
questions.explanation: Due to better preservation of autoregulation, equivalent reduction in CMR and smoother recovery, sevoflurane is preferred over isoflurane as an inhalational agent of choice for neurosurgery.

questions.question: Which IV anesthetics are most potent in decreasing CBF, CMR, CMO2 and ICT?
questions.explanation: Barbiturates, propofol, etomidate and benzodiazepines decrease CBF, CMR, CMO2 and ICT. Barbiturates are most potent in this aspect.

questions.question: What is the effect of opioids on CBF in neurosurgical patients?

options.B: Decrease CMR and CMO2 but increase CBF (directly acting cerebral vasodilator) and hence ICT

questions.explanation: Opioids decrease CMR and CMO2 but increases CBF (directly acting cerebral vasodilator) and hence ICT. This increase in CBF is significant when blood brain barrier is disrupted.

questions.question: By how much can Ketamine increase CBF and ICT?
questions.explanation: Ketamine increases CBF and ICT very significantly (up to 60%). Ketamine also increases CMR and CMO2.

questions.question: What is the effect of succinylcholine on ICT?
questions.explanation: Succinylcholine increases the ICT.

questions.question: Do non-depolarizing muscle relaxants cross the blood brain barrier?

options.B: No, they do not cross the blood brain barrier

questions.explanation: Non-depolarizing muscle relaxants do not cross the blood brain barrier.

questions.question: What is the effect of lignocaine on CBF, CMR and CMO2?

options.B: Decreases CBF, CMR, CMO2 (but at toxic doses can induce seizure and increase CMR)

questions.explanation: Lignocaine decreases CBF, CMR, CMO2 (but at toxic doses it can induce seizure and can increase CMR).

questions.question: How do vasopressors (Mephentermine, Ephedrine, Epinephrine) affect CBF?

options.B: Increase CBF indirectly by increasing cerebral perfusion pressure

questions.explanation: Vasopressors increase CBF indirectly by increasing cerebral perfusion pressure.

questions.question: Why should glucose containing solutions be avoided in neurosurgery?
questions.explanation: As hyperglycemia can precipitate cerebral edema therefore, Glucose containing solutions should be avoided.

questions.question: What is the best policy for fluid management in neurosurgical patients?

options.B: Alternate ringer and normal saline liter by liter

questions.explanation: The best policy is to alternate ringer and normal saline liter by liter. Ringer lactate is hypoosmolar and large quantities can aggravate cerebral edema. Normal saline in large quantities can cause hyperchloremic metabolic acidosis.

questions.question: Is routine use of hypothermia recommended in neurosurgery?

options.B: No, routine use of hypothermia is not recommended

questions.explanation: Routine use of hypothermia is not recommended.

questions.question: What is the optimal blood sugar range to maintain in neurosurgical patients?
questions.explanation: Both hyperglycemia as well as hypoglycemia can be detrimental therefore, blood sugar should be best maintained between 140 and 180 mg%.

questions.question: What is the most important monitor for neurosurgical patients?
questions.explanation: Most important is Capnography. Hypercapnia is the most potent cerebral vasodilator therefore, can significantly increase the ICT.

questions.question: What is the beneficial range of pCO2 during monitoring in neurosurgery?
questions.explanation: Moderate hypocapnia (pCO2 between 25 and 30 mm Hg) is beneficial by decreasing ICT however excessive hypocapnia (pCO2 < 25 mm Hg) can cause cerebral ischemia.

questions.question: Why should hypoxia be avoided during pulse oximetry monitoring in neurosurgery?

options.B: It increases ICT by causing cerebral vasodilation

questions.explanation: Hypoxia should be avoided as it increases ICT by causing cerebral vasodilation.

questions.question: What is essential for smooth emergence in neurosurgical patients?

options.B: Emergence free of coughing, straining and hypertension

questions.explanation: Smooth emergence, i.e. emergence free of coughing, straining, and hypertension is must in neurosurgical patients as these can increase ICT and cause massive bleeding.

questions.question: What is the best method to achieve smooth emergence in neurosurgery?

options.B: Inject lignocaine before extubation, continue inhalational (at least N2O) till dressing and use beta blockers

questions.explanation: The best possible methods to achieve smooth emergence is to inject lignocaine before extubation, continue inhalational (at least N2O) till dressing is over and use beta blockers (Esmolol, Labetalol) if necessary.

questions.question: What is the target CPP (MAP-ICP) to maintain during neurosurgery?
questions.explanation: The aim should be to maintain CPP (MAP-ICP) between 70 and 100 mm Hg not only in intraoperative period but also in post-operative period for at least 2-3 days.

questions.question: Are central neuraxial blocks contraindicated in raised ICT?
questions.explanation: Central neuraxial blocks are absolutely contraindicated in raised ICT.

questions.question: Which induction agent is preferred over thiopentone for neurosurgery?
questions.explanation: Although Thiopentone has more favorable effect on CMR and ICT than propofol but still due to shorter half-life propofol is preferred over thiopentone for induction.

questions.question: Why can intubation be problematic in neurosurgical patients?

options.B: Intubation can significantly increase ICT due to response to laryngoscopy

questions.explanation: Intubation can significantly increase the ICT therefore, response to laryngoscopy and intubation must be blunted with lignocaine, adequate depth and beta blockers.

questions.question: Is Suxamethonium absolutely contraindicated for neurosurgery?

options.B: Not absolutely contraindicated - can be safely used for rapid sequence induction or difficult airway

questions.explanation: Suxamethonium is not absolutely contraindicated for neurosurgery and can be safely used if there is clear indication like rapid sequence induction or possibility of difficult airway.

questions.question: What agents should be used for maintenance if ICP is too high and surgical field too tight?

options.B: IV agents only - inhalational agents should be avoided

questions.explanation: If ICP is too high to be controlled or surgical field is too tight that inhalational agents should be avoided and anesthesia is maintained on IV agents.

questions.question: What should be assumed about all head injury patients regarding cervical injury?

options.B: All patients should be assumed to be having cervical injury (incidence up to 15%) until proved otherwise

questions.explanation: All patients of head injury should be assumed to be having cervical injury (incidence up to 15%) until proved otherwise.

questions.question: When should nasal intubation NOT be performed in head injury?

options.B: Unless basal skull fracture has been ruled out

questions.explanation: Nasal intubation should not be performed unless basal skull fracture has been ruled out.

questions.question: What is the best method for assessing neurologic status in head injury?
questions.explanation: The best method for assessing neurologic status is modified Glasgow Coma scale.

questions.question: What is the normal Glasgow Coma Scale score?
questions.explanation: Normal score is 15/15. Severe head injury is stated to be present if score is 7 or less.

questions.question: A GCS score of 7 or less persisting for more than 6 hours is associated with what mortality?
questions.explanation: Severe head injury is stated to be present if score is 7 or less and if persist for more than 6 hours is associated with more than 35% mortality.

questions.question: Why is DIC commonly associated with head injury?

options.B: Due to release of brain thromboplastin

questions.explanation: DIC is commonly associated with head injury due to release of brain thromboplastin and it must be ruled out in preoperative period.

questions.question: What is the most sensitive tool to detect venous air embolism (VAE)?
questions.explanation: The most sensitive tool to detect VAE is transesophageal echocardiography (TEE). It can detect as low as 0.25 mL of air.

questions.question: At what volume of entertained air does VAE become clinically significant?
questions.explanation: VAE can become clinically significant if the volume of entertained air > 100 mL (however death has been reported with as low as 5 mL of air).

questions.question: What is the incidence of VAE in posterior fossa craniotomies?
questions.explanation: The incidence of venous air embolism (VAE) can be as high as 20-40% in posterior fossa craniotomies however, fortunately the incidence of clinically significant VAE is very less.

questions.question: What is the first step in treatment of venous air embolism?

options.B: Stop nitrous oxide and start 100% oxygen (N2O can expand the size of air embolus)

questions.explanation: Stop nitrous oxide and start 100% oxygen. Nitrous oxide can expand the size of air embolus.

questions.question: What is the sensitivity order for detection of air embolism in decreasing order?

options.B: TEE > Precordial Doppler > PAP, ETCO2, ET nitrogen > CVP > ECG > Clinical signs

questions.explanation: The sensitivity in decreasing order is: TEE > Precordial Doppler > PAP, ETCO2, ET nitrogen > CVP > ECG > clinical signs.

questions.question: What are the clinical signs of venous air embolism?

options.B: Hypotension, tachycardia, cyanosis, mill wheel murmur, cardiovascular collapse

questions.explanation: Clinical signs include hypotension, tachycardia, cyanosis, mill wheel murmur, cardiovascular collapse. Clinical signs appear late in venous air embolism.

questions.question: After stroke surgery, when is the period of maximal vasospasm risk that should be avoided?
questions.explanation: After stroke surgery should be avoided in period of maximal vasospasm risk (4-14 days) as far as possible.
